comments: Tommy credits the Gary Numan session to Gary Numan's Tubeway Army. Studio versions of these tracks would later appear on Gary's solo album “The Pleasure Principle” which was still being recorded at this point and wouldn’t be released until September.

sessions.details: recorded 1976-01-26 for John Peel (Session #1). First TX 1976-02-24.
recorded 1979-05-29 for John Peel. First TX 1979-06-25.

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Hi there and good evening. I'm Tommy Vance and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight an archive session recorded in January 1976 by Lone Star.
We've got Gary Newman's Tube by Army, the latest session by them.
And also a special set recorded live in America, never broadcast, by Ted Nugent.
You can hear classic cuts from Jimi Hendrix, Sid Barrett, Status Quo, Frigid Pink, among others, and new releases by Interview, Avery and Pat Travers.
Of course, we've got the Friday Night Connection.
But tonight we start with something brand new from a forthcoming album called Mirrors.
This is Blue Oyster Cult.
Bangers and all the Quo armies around the country, Status Quo and a real old classic that came out yonks ago called Down Down.
Prior to that, something from an album that's going to come out in about a fortnight's time, we believe.
It's out in America at the moment.
The album is called Mirrors.
The track you heard was Dr. Music.
And the band, of course, was Blue Oyster Cult.
Ah, and what an album.
A couple of people who will be pleased about that are Jo Hardingham, I think it is.
She calls herself a definite miss.
She lives in Macclesfield.
She says we don't play enough B.O.C.
Well, we hopefully just put that right.
And so, indeed, the Sterling Gow of number two, Jasmine Terrace in Aberdeen.
Blue Oyster Cult, when we get the new stuff, we'll play it.
There's an album coming out in September,
which is going to be by Gary Newman's Two-Boy Army.
The title of it will be Pleasure Principle.
But here, as a preview, is a track that they laid down
at the end of May in the BBC Studios.
This is Conversation.
It's from the album Midnight Lightning.
The track was Machine Gun.
And I played it specially for Tug Tugwell,
who at the moment I think is in Nottingham or thereabouts,
from his loving wife Mandy, who misses him
and hopes he's in really good condition.
And also I got a great letter from a lady by the name of Mary Hawthorne.
She lives in Redditch.
She calls herself a loony English teacher with a love of music,
commonly called the caftan freak.
She wanted me to dedicate some Jimi Hendrix
to the foundation technical class of 78 and 79,
thanks to Chris and Whitey and Mike and Dave and Martin and Stephen Garrell
and Scoo, S-C-H-O, I think it is, and Mac and Robert and Deli
and Tim and Scotty and especially Nick Dunn
For the friendship and the trust in lending his Jimi Hendrix records to her.
I'll miss you, she says.
Signed, Mary, the Kaftan freak, teacher and friend.
Last week, I made a horrendous mistake.
I forgot to back-announce a piece of music.
It was the piece of music that I introduced as being something that was going to please people.
It was by Tangerine Dream.
It was from the album called Force Majeure.
Terrible French.
And the track was Cloudburst Flight.
and on behalf of all the people who wrote in and said,
what a fool I am, just let me tell you that you're right.
And now here's something by the Dixon House Band.
Getting into when I heard it first this afternoon,
but eventually I did and I trust you did as well just now when you heard it.
It's my interview, it's from a brand new album which is called Big Oceans.
It's just come out on Virgin and the track was Shipyards.
And before that you heard something by a gentleman,
well, written by a gentleman called E. Dixon House
and the band are called the Dixon House Band.
It's a new single. It's out on Infinity, and its title was Sooner or Later.
It's 25 minutes now before 11 o'clock here on the Friday Rock Show.
Via the telephone came this message for a lady by the name of Jane of Hitchin in Harts
from a gentleman by the name of Tony.
Jane, I've got to remind you of next Tuesday.
Do not forget.
Right, let's go now to our archive session recorded on the 26th of January 1976.
It was the first session that this band ever did.
It was even done before they started to record, to the best of our recollection, their first album.
Sadly missed, they are. Sadly missed, indeed.
Without an interruption, the first session ever recorded for Radio 1 by Lone Star.
I nearly fell out of the car the first time I heard that.
It was broadcast on the Saturday show in 1976.
Lone Star, sadly missed.
If only they'd stayed together, I think they would have been up there with Rush and Boston and everybody else.
they would have been a massive band great session produced by tony wilson the tracks he heard what
she said a million stars and flying in the real this week we heard that kenny driscoll the original
singer has reformed the band with new members and they have just started gigging there's a possible

of Wallasey, and Mr. Chris Peel
of Scoresby, Doncaster,
South Yorkshire.
Well worth rebroadcasting it was.
It's 5 to 11. Here's
Gary Newman's Tubeway Army and Films.

Love Songs. They did it for a lot

and ask, where is Sid? Straight answer
to that is, I don't really know.
we've got his music and you want to hear it on the radio so we play it every once in a while

the same. It was a soundtrack.

Well done, Richard. Ten pound record

But Richard's really had to be the best.
And it's very, very varied.
And it starts like this.
Well, I dreamed I saw

The title of the songs they sang, whoever wrote them
Well, at the moment, it's for Richard Haslam of Belfast,

and it's for you to find out.

If you ever feel the urge to write to us, please do,

and we do take note of your suggestions.
If there are any archive sessions you'd like us to delve around and find,

just write to us.
